key_insight: This survey shows that many reinforcement learning algorithms can be understood as fixed-point, optimization, and stochastic approximation methods acting on Bellman operators and value functions.
engineering_relevance: It gives practitioners a mathematical toolkit for designing RL systems with convergence guarantees, sample-complexity awareness, and principled handling of constraints and function approximation.
method: Bellman-operator analysis with contraction, monotonicity, and fixed-point theory for value/policy iteration and temporal-difference learning
Optimization view via stochastic approximation, martingales, convex duality, and mirror/proximal regularization
Function approximation and off-policy/CMDP analysis using concentration bounds, stabilization techniques, and operator/variational formulations
evidence_signal: The paper is a theory-focused survey, emphasizing finite-sample bounds, asymptotic results, and convergence/rate analyses rather than new experimental benchmarks.
watch_item: Practical performance can depend strongly on data dependence, mixing, approximation error, and off-policy instability, so theoretical guarantees may be sensitive to model and sampling assumptions.
